text:
The swift progression of cloud computing technologies
has led to a significant transformation in data sharing
landscape. Nowadays, both individuals and enterprises
are increasingly reliant on cloud storage platforms for
uploading and disseminating data.  However,  since
cloud  service providers(CSPs) may not  always  be
perceived as fully reliable, and given the high sensitivity
of much of the data stored in the cloud, ensuring access
control in cloud storage is crucial.
In public cloud storage, ciphertext-policy attribute-
based encryption(CP-ABE) provides an effective ap-
proach to achieve access control [1]. CP-ABE enables
fine-grained access control by embedding access struc-
ture directly into the encrypted data and associating

attributes with decryption keys. This allows data own-
ers to specify access policy in encryption, and decryp-
tion is only possible when data user’s attributes match
the access policy defined in the ciphertext.
However, Attribute-Based Encryption(ABE) upends
the traditional trust model of public-key encryption by
relying on a single trusted authority that maintains a
long-term master secret key to issue decryption keys [2].
If the central authority is compromised and its secret
key is obtained by an adversary, the adversary would be
able to decrypt all ciphertexts within the system. The
proposal of decentralized ABE still relies on multiple
authorities, and therefore, it fails to completely address
this  issue  [3].  To address the key-escrow problem,
Hohenberger  et  al.  [4]  proposed a  registered ABE
scheme without any authority that allows users  to
,  Vol. ??,  No. ??,  ????

2  D. Cai
generate secret keys on their own and then register the
associated public key with a key curator along with their
attributes. The key curator combines the public keys
of different users into a single master public key. Users
may need to obtain auxiliary decryption keys from the
key curator, which they then combine with their own
secret keys to decrypt. The key curator is transparent
and does not maintain secrets,  allowing  for public
auditing, as both registration and update algorithms
are deterministic.  Although a malicious key curator
could register attributes that allow the decryption of
all ciphertexts, such behavior can always be detected
by an external auditor. By utilizing a decentralized and
immutable ledger, blockchain [5] can provide auditable
records of all operations performed by the key curator.
This  ensures  that any malicious behavior,  such as
the registration of unauthorized attributes or wrong
computation, can be detected by public verifier. The
tamper-resistant nature of  blockchain enhances  the
integrity of the system by enabling the verification and
traceability of all  interactions without reliance on a
central authority.
Alternatively, both pairing-based ABE [2, 6, 7] and
registered ABE  [4, 8]  suffer  from high decryption
overhead,  which poses  a  significant  challenge  for
lightweight devices.  To address this  issue  in ABE,
Green et al.  [9] proposed an outsourced decryption
scheme for attribute-based encryption(OABE), which
significantly reduces the computational overhead for
users without  revealing sensitive  information about
the original data.  In their scheme, user generates a
transformation key (TK) and a retrieve key (RK) from
the attribute private key (SK) and sends the TK and
ciphertext (CT) to the decryption cloud server (DCS)
for partial decryption, which transforms the ciphertext
into an ElGamal ciphertext [10]. The user then uses the
RK to decrypt the ElGamal ciphertext and retrieve the
plaintext.
Since the DCS may not perform the computation
honestly to save computation resources, many verifiable
OABE schemes have been proposed in the literature
[11–15].

However, many previous  schemes  fail  to
achieve the properties of  exemptibility and fairness.
The exemptibility property ensures that the DCS is
not falsely accused of malicious behavior if it returns
a correct  transformed ciphertext, while the  fairness
property ensures that the DCS gets paid if and only
if  it returns a correct result.  Cui et al.  [16] propose
a  functional  encryption with  payable  outsourced
decryption (FEPOD) scheme based on blockchain [5],
which achieves these two properties without relying
on a trusted authority.  Ge et al.  [17]  eliminates
redundant  information while achieving exemptibility
and fairness,  but outsources heavy computation to
smart contracts, which will cause high gas usage and
performance bottlenecks.
Despite  the  success  of OABE,  to  the  best  of
our knowledge,  there  is no outsourcing decryption
The Computer Journal

et al.
scheme has been proposed for registered ABE. This
gap motivates our work,  as we aim to develop first
outsourced decryption scheme for registered attribute-
based encryption(ORABE) which not only maintains
the security and privacy of the data but also achieves
verifiability,  exemptibility,  fairness and decentralized
outsourcing.
1.1.  Motivation and Contribution
While registered ABE tackles the key-escrow problem
upon traditional ABE by removing central authority,
it  requires  robust  auditing  to ensure  the  integrity
of  key  registration and update  processes,  as  the
transparent key curator still plays a critical  role  in
the system.  Additionally,  it still  suffers  from high
decryption overhead like ABE, making it unsuitable
for lightweight devices. Many verifiable OABE schemes
have been proposed to address this issue on ABE and
several OABE schemes  [16, 17] achieve exemptibility
but outsource heavy computation to blockchain, leading
to high gas fees and performance issues. To the best
of our knowledge,  there is no outsourced decryption
scheme currently existing for registered ABE. This gap
motivates our work to develop the first outsourced
decryption scheme for registered ABE, which aims to
reduce decryption overhead while ensuring security,
verifiability,  exemptibility,  and  fairness  based  on
blockchain. In summary, the contributions of this paper
are threefold.
•  We propose  the first  fully auditable  registered
attribute-based encryption with outsourced de-
cryption scheme based on blockchain, which re-
duces the decryption overhead to only one group
exponentiation operation without pairing opera-
tion.
•  We achieve verifiability of  transform ciphertext
by utilizing a verifiable tag mechanism [13] and
achieve exemptibility which enables  the honest
DCS to escape from wrong claims through NIZK
fraud proof under optimistic assumption. Besides,
our scheme also achieves fairness and decentralized
outsourcing, and the payment can be processed by
cryptocurrency based on blockchain.
•  We  implement  a  concrete  auditable  registered
ABE scheme with reliable and payable outsourced
decryption, evaluate its feasibility and performance
on  Ethereum,  and  show  its  application  in
decentralized finance(DeFi).
1.2.  Related Work
1.2.1.  Outsourced Decryption of Attribute-Based En-
cryption
Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE) was first introduced
as fuzzy identity-based encryption by Sahai and Waters
in [18].  ABE schemes are generally classified into
two categories: Ciphertext-Policy ABE (CP-ABE) and
,  Vol. ??,  No. ??,  ????

Auditable and Reliable O
Key-Policy ABE (KP-ABE) [6], depending on whether
the access policy is embedded in the ciphertext or
the user’s private key.  A user  can decrypt  only
if  the set of attributes satisfies the access policy of
ciphertext.  However,  the decryption in the ABE is
too expensive, and this problem is especially acute for
resource limited devices such as mobile devices, which
greatly hinders its real-world adoption. To mitigate this
issue, Green et al. [9] proposed outsourcing the majority
of the decryption to third party without revealing the
plaintext. The pioneer work [9] can append a hash of
ciphertext randomness as tag to check the correctness
of transformation. Consequently, Lai et al. [11] extend
the original ABE model with outsourced decryption to
achieve verifiability. Their approach involved attaching
an additional ciphertext of a random message and a
tag to each original ciphertext which double the size
of the original ABE ciphertext and the transformation
cost.  Based on the scheme [11], Qin et al.  [13] and
Lin et al.  [14]  aimed to enhance  the efficiency of
OABE. Qin et al. [13] managed to reduce the ciphertext
size on both the server and client sides by half and
achieved faster decryption than the scheme proposed
in [11]. They also introduced a method to transform any
ABE scheme with outsourced decryption into one that
supports verifiable outsourced decryption.

Lin et al.
[14] proposed a more efficient and generic construction
of ABE with verifiable outsourced decryption based
on an attribute-based key encapsulation mechanism, a
symmetric-key encryption scheme, and a commitment
scheme.  Furthermore, Li  et al.  [12]  introduced the
notion of ABE scheme that support both outsourced
encryption and decryption,  but  only  supports  the
threshold access policy.  Following their work, Ma et
al.  [19]  extended it to accommodate any monotonic
access policy and established a more stronger security
framework compared to [11].  But the exemptibility
they claimed is not achieved since the user may reveal
an incorrect private key and accuse the decryption
cloud server  falsely.  None of  the aforementioned
schemes can achieve both exemptibility and fairness. To
tackle this issue, Cui et al. [16] introduced an payable
OABE scheme that  leverages blockchain technology
to achieve exemptibility and fairness.  However,  it
still  introduces  redundant  information.  Ge  et  al.
[17] eliminated redundant  information and mitigated
heavy computation problem of smart contracts through
decomposing to pieces of meta computations, but their
solution still  results  in high gas  consumption and
performance limitations. Furthermore, due to the lack
of support for pairing computation in native Ethereum
[20],  this  scheme  is  incompatible with Ethereum
platform.
1.2.2.  Registered Attribute-Based Encryption
Registered Attribute-Based Encryption has  recently
been introduced as a significant extension of ABE,
The Computer Journal

RABE Using Blockchain  3
aimed at addressing the key escrow problem [4].  It
replaces the trusted key-issuer who maintains a master
secret  key  to  issue decryption keys  in  traditional
ABE with a transparent and untrusted key curator
to aggregate users’ public keys.  In a registered ABE
system,  users generate  their own public/secret key
pairs  independently,  similar  to standard public-key
encryption.  They subsequently register their public
keys with a key curator,  along with the attributes
set.  The key curator  aggregates  these pairs  into
a compact master public key.  A user  encrypts a
message under an access policy using the master public
key to generate a ciphertext.  Decryption  is only
possible for users who possess an attributes set that
satisfies the access policy, using a helper decryption
key from the key curator and their own secret key.
Hohenberger  et  al.  [4]  introduced  the  concept  of
registered ABE and provided a construction based
on assumptions over composite-order pairing groups.
Their scheme supports all monotone Boolean formula
policies and accommodates a bounded number of users
L. However, it relies on a structured common reference
string (CRS) whose size scales quadratically with the
number of users L and linearly with the size of the
attribute universe. Subsequently, several constructions
of registered ABE have been proposed, which enhance
both the  functionality and security of  the original
scheme [8,21–26]. There are some constructions require
non-black-box use of  cryptography (i.e.,obfuscation-
based or witness-encryption-based approaches) [8, 21–
23, 25],  and pairing-based constructions  that  only
require black-box use of cryptography [8, 24–26]. The
pairing-based constructions  are more  efficient,  but
many of them [4, 24–26] had a CRS whose size was
quadratic in the number of users, and for the schemes
that supported general Boolean formulas, also linear
in the number of attributes. Grag et al.  [8] reducing
the CRS size to nearly linear in the number of users
1+o(1)
L(i.e. L  ) with progression-free sets, which makes
the scheme more practical.  But  the pairing-based
construction of  [8]  still  suffers  from high decryption
overhead. Therefore, we build upon their construction
to develop an outsourced decryption scheme aimed
at minimizing the decryption burden for users while
achieving verifiability, exemptibility, and fairness.
1.2.3.  Applications of Optimistic Fraud Proof
The fraud proof operates under an optimistic assump-
tion, defaulting to accept statement unless proven oth-
erwise.  It includes a challenge window, during which
challengers may initiate disputes to demonstrate the
statement’s invalidity.  In contrast, the validity proof
offers evidence confirming the correctness of the state-
ment, with acceptance granted only when its accuracy
is definitively established under a pessimistic assump-
tion. Optimistic rollups [27], [28], [29], [30], [31], [32], a
key solution for Ethereum’s scalability, assumes that

In this section, we describe our system architecture,
threat model, and security definition.
3.1.  System Architecture
Our system consists of seven entities, namely Trusted
Setup Parties(TSPs), Key Curator(KC),  InterPlane-
tary File System(IPFS), Data Owners(DOs), Data
,  Vol. ??,  No. ??,  ????

6  D. Cai
Users(DUs), Decryption Cloud Server(DCS) Network
and Public Verifier(PV). These seven entities are de-
fined as follows:
•  TSPs are responsible for one-time trusted setup
through decentralized protocol  [38]  to generate
common reference string crs.
•  KC is responsible for aggregating the public keys
registered by users and generating master public
key and helper decryption keys for all users.
•  IPFS is a distributed file system to store data
without a central server.  It stores auxiliary data
and ciphertexts in our system.
•  DOs use specified access structure to encrypt data,
upload the ciphertext to IPFS, and publish the
corresponding ciphertext tag on blockchain.
•  DUs  publish  outsourced  decryption  task  on
blockchain, get transform ciphertext from it, and
finish final decryption with secret key.  Besides,
DUs can generate fraud proof to accuse dishonest
DCS.
•  DCS  network  consists  of  many  servers  that
compete  for tasks.  DCSs get ciphertexts  from
IPFS,  request helper decryption key from KC,
compute  and  submit  transform ciphertext  to
blockchain.
•  PV verifies fraud proofs submitted by DUs and
publishes the verification result on blockchain.
FIGURE 1: Overview of happy case.
Fig 1 illustrates the overview of happy case of our
scheme, which consists of the following steps:
•  TSPs execute trusted setup ceremony and generate
common reference string CRS.
•  DU generates private key and public key locally,
then registers their public key(pku) and attributes
set to KC.
•  KC firstly verifies DU’s public key(pk ).  If pk
u  u
is valid, KC computes and updates auxiliary data
on IPFS, and publishes new state hash hash(aux)
and public key hash hash(pku) on blockchain.
The Computer Journal

et al.
•  DO encrypts sensitive data using registered ABE
under Key Encapsulation Mechanism(KEM) and
sends ciphertext to IPFS.
•  DO publishes  the associated ciphertext  tag to
blockchain so that DU can check the correctness
of transform ciphertext with the tag.
•  DU publishes  outsourced  decryption  task  on
blockchain.
•  DCS observes  outsourced decryption task and
downloads corresponding ciphertext and auxiliary
data from IPFS.
•  DCS  transforms  the  original  ciphertext  and
submits the transform ciphertext to blockchain.
•  DU gets the result from blockchain and finishes the
decryption locally.
•  DCS claims  task reward from blockchain after
challenge window.
FIGURE 2: Overview of dispute phase.
After the final decryption, DU could check whether
the decrypted plaintext matches the ciphertext tag.  If
not, DU can enter the dispute phase which involves four
more steps as shown in Fig 2:
•  DU generates  fraud  proof  to  accuse  DCS’s
malicious behavior and publish it on blockchain.
•  PV gets fraud proof from blockchain and verifies
its validity.
•  PV publishes the verification result on blockchain.
•  Smart contract will return DU’s deposit if the fraud
proof is valid.
Note that the optimistic assumption supposes that both
parties are honest unless proven otherwise. The dispute
phase and public verifier are needed only when one
player crashes or attempts to cheat under our optimistic
assumption.
3.2.  Threat Model
In our scheme, DOs and IPFS are fully trusted. TSPs
execute one-time trusted setup through decentralized
protocol, the setup is secure as long as any single party
participates honestly [38]. KC and PV are semi-honest
and transparent, which means they maintain no secret
and perform computation honestly,  but are curious
about secrets. DCSs are malicious, indicating that they
may try to obtain task reward by submitting incorrect
results deliberately.  Considering that DUs intend to
propose invalid fraud proof  in order not to pay, we
assume DU to be malicious as well.

We first present slotted registered ABE and registered
ABE scheme with outsourced decryption based on
registered ABE scheme in [8]. Then we describe a NIZK
fraud proof mechanism under optimistic assumption
to achieve  exemptibility.  Finally,  we present  the
concrete smart contract design for our auditable and
fair outsourced decryption scheme.

4.3.  NIZK Fraud Proof Mechanism
Fraud proof,  also known as  fault proof,  is a proof
submitted by a challenger that challenges the previous
result under optimistic assumption.  If  the decrypt
algorithm outputs  ,  it indicates that the transform
⊥
ciphertext  is not correct and DU needs to generate
fraud proof to accuse DCS and get the deposit back.
The fraud proof generation and verification based on
the Discrete Logarithm Equality(DLEQ) NIZK proof is
defined as follows:

Unfortunately,  this  verify algorithm can not  be

t
So, we introduce a semi-honest and transparent public
verifier to verify the fraud proof and publish verification
result on blockchain. Ge et al.  [17] added precompile
contract to native Ethereum to support pairing and G
t
computation on their own blockchain.  If reduce the
reliance on native Ethereum security, we can integrate
their approach to eliminate the role for semi-honest
public verifier and enable the verification of fraud proof
directly through smart contracts.  In this work, we
make a trade-off and prioritize the security of native
Ethereum.  Note that in the registered ABE scheme,
this public verifier role can also be played by KC, who
is semi-honest and transparent.

4.4.  Smart Contract
Fig 3 illustrates the BA-ORABE smart contract of
our scheme, which manages the whole procedure of
user registration, ciphertext tag publication, outsourced
decryption, and public verification of fraud proofs. The
addresses of KC and PV are recorded in contract once
it is deployed.
In our ORABE scheme, each DU first registers itself
to KC. On receiving a pku from a DU, KC allocates
a suitable ctr  to DU and updates aux data.  To
make sure that KC’s behavior is fully auditable, KC
should PublishState when it finishes registering DU.
The state data indicates the input and output of the
registration, which consists of  ctr,  hash(pku),  and
hash(aux).
Ciphertexts are  stored  in IPFS,  and DO should
PublishTag  after  uploading  ciphertext.  Smart
contract will record the index of ciphertext in IPFS and
its relative tag. The index is used to stipulate which  2

in the public key pku of DU.

Meanwhile, the invoker also

2) idtask := hash(msg.sender, ctTag, $reward, Tnow)

msg.sender, ctTag: ctTag, ctr: ctru, reward: $reward, sta-
tus: Published)
4) Return idtask
SubmitResult: Receive (idtask, ct′) as input, where idtask
is task identifier, and ct′ are partially decrypted result.
1) Assert Tasks[taskId].status == Published
2) Tasks[taskId].submitter = msg.sender
3) Tasks[taskId].status = Submitted
4) Tasks[taskId].result = ct′
5) Tasks[taskId].challengeDeadline = Tnow + Tp
ClaimReward: Receive (idtask) as input.
1) Assert Tasks[taskId].status == Submitted
2) Assert Tasks[taskId].submitter == msg.sender
3) Assert Tnow > Tasks[taskId].challengeDeadline
4) Tasks[taskId].status = Completed
5) Transfer(msg.sender, Tasks[taskId].reward)
————————(auxiliary functions)————————
DisposeReward: Receive (idtask, b) as input. This func-
tion dispose reward automatically according to b.
1) reward := Tasks[taskId].reward
2) If (b == 1):
Transfer(Tasks[taskId].publisher, reward)
3) If (b == 0):
Transfer(Tasks[taskId].submitter, reward)

ciphertext to be decrypted, while the tag is used by DU
to check the correctness of partially decrypted result
and used by PV to verify the validity of fraud proof.
Regarding outsourced decryption, DU first publishes
an outsourced decryption task by CreateTask. Note
that each task contains some cryptocurrency as task re-
ward. DCS retrieves the corresponding ciphertext from
IPFS, performs transformation(partial decryption), and
SubmitResult to the blockchain. DU completes the
decryption locally upon receiving the result.  In the
happy case, DCS could ClaimReward after the chal-
lenge window. While in the dispute phase, DU could
generate a fraud proof and PublishFraudProof dur-
ing the challenge window.  PV verifies the proof, and
PublishVerificationResult on blockchain. The aux-
iliary function DisposeReward is invoked by Pub-
lishFraudProof.  It automatically sends the task re-
ward back to the DU if the proof is valid, and to the
DCS otherwise.

5.4.  Fairness
Theorem 5.5. The registered ABE scheme with out-
sourced decryption based on Ethereum keeps fairness.
Proof. The fairness of our scheme ensures that the DCS
gets paid if and only if it returns a correct result. The
DU must deposit tokens  into the smart contract to
submit the outsourced decryption task.  If the DCS
submits the correct result,  it will receive the reward
at the end of the challenge window, which is ensured by
exemptibility.  Otherwise, the malicious behavior will
be detected by DU, which is ensured by verifiability,
and DCS get nothing but pay for the transaction gas
fee for malicious behavior. Thus the proposed scheme
achieves the fairness property as long as the verifiability,
the exemptibility, and Ethereum consensus protocol is
guaranteed.

6.1.  Theoretical Analysis
Table  1  shows  the  comparison  of  some  existing
registered ABE schemes and our scheme.  Since our
scheme is based on [8], the parameters, except for the
decryption cost, are identical to those of [8]. And the
parameter sizes are better than other schemes. Besides,
our outsourced decryption significantly reduces the final
decryption cost to a constant level without any pairing
operation,  requiring only one group exponentiation
operation.
Table 2 shows the comparison of the previous OABE
schemes and our scheme in terms of authority, verifia-
bility, exemptibility, fairness, decentralized outsourcing,
and final decryption cost. Our scheme is the only one
that does not rely on a trusted authority and achieves
the lowest decryption overhead among all the compared
schemes. While most schemes ensure verifiability, only
our scheme and [17] provide exemptibility, fairness, and
decentralized outsourcing capabilities.
6.2.  Experimental Evaluation
We conducted experiments  in three aspects:  slotted
registered ABE computation cost, zero-knowledge fraud
proof  cost,  and smart contract gas usage.  In our
experiment,  the access policy is set as an AND gate
of attributes and its size varies from 5 to 100, with a
step size of 5.
6.2.1.  Outsourced  Decryption  Slotted  Registered
ABE(sORABE)  vs.  Slotted  Registered
ABE(sRABE)
As we use the slotted registered ABE to registered ABE
transformation [4], which constructs registered ABE
through multiple slotted registered ABE instances, We
only compared the basic slotted scheme here.  We
conducted experiment and performance analysis on the
computation cost comparison between sRABE scheme
in  [8]  and our  sRABE with outsourced decryption
scheme, excluding the computational cost of symmetric

In  this  paper,  we  proposed BA-ORABE,  a  fully
auditable  registered  attribute-based  encryption
scheme with reliable outsourced decryption based on
blockchain,  and then presented security proof of  its
static security,  verifiability,  and exemptibility.  Our
proposed scheme achieves verifiability through verifi-
able tag mechanism and exemptibility through NIZK
proof. Furthermore, We extended fully auditable prop-
erty and realized fairness and decentralized outsourcing
based on public blockchain. Finally, we compared the
theoretical  complexity of BA-ORABE with current
existing schemes and implemented and evaluated it on
Ethereum to demonstrate efficiency and feasibility. We
also showed that our scheme provides significant ad-
vantages in DeFi application, which could be explored
further in future work.
